clean_story:
Algorithms as attention magnets. Memes act like cheat codes.

The term "meme" comes from Richard Dawkins’ 1976 idea of cultural units spreading like genes—but today’s memes are digital parasites, optimized to exploit algorithms. Platforms like TikTok or Instagram rank content by engagement: likes, shares, comments. Memes pack visual puns, nostalgia, or shock into seconds, triggering rapid-fire reactions. Algorithms mistake this for "quality," so they push memes harder.

Example: The "Distracted Boyfriend" stock photo became a template in 2017. Users remixed it endlessly (e.g., "Distracted GF watching Bitcoin crash"). Each variant reused the same structure, training algorithms to favor familiar patterns over originality. Result? A feedback loop where simplicity beats depth.

Memes also game dwell time—the seconds you pause on a post. A chaotic edit (e.g., "Surreal Memes" with absurdist captions) forces a double-take, boosting metrics. Platforms then assume you want more surrealism, narrowing your feed.

Three related subjects are viral loop mechanics, attention economy traps, and how stock images become memes.
